📕Artificial Intelligence: A Philosophical and Civilizational Challenge📕

Alexander Dugin
Host :  Today we have some rather challenging topics on the agenda. I'd like to discuss how artificial intelligence and its products are entering our lives and how they are changing them. What should we be wary of? After all, for many today, AI is practically their worst nightmare: being "digitally stigmatized" or facing algorithmic aggression online has become more frightening than actual threats.

On the other hand, there are direct instructions from the president and statements from top government officials: by 2030, all enterprises must actively implement these technologies. And now we're seeing the first reports: the Ministry of Health claims that digitalization and AI assistants are helping combat staff shortages and making life easier for doctors and staff. Electronic document management is already commonplace, and such steps by the government seem encouraging. This perspective is increasingly being discussed in healthcare.

But how should we really look at this? Is this a long-awaited relief from our reality, or something truly frightening, hiding behind the façade of convenience? How do you see this situation?

Alexander Dugin : I think the problem of artificial intelligence is the main challenge of our time. And it's not just a technological one. It's not just a question of how many workers it will replace, who it will fire, or who it will make redundant. Artificial intelligence creates colossal threats of an entirely different kind. It's no coincidence that Trump said the arms race is now unfolding not so much in the nuclear sphere as in the field of AI. Whoever controls artificial intelligence—if it can be controlled at all, and that's a major philosophical problem—controls the world.

Today, the outcome of wars is decided by controlling the public consciousness. This has been clear for a hundred years, if not earlier. What the sociologist Émile Durkheim called "collective consciousness" is the key to power. By controlling it, one can manipulate not only people's bodies, forcing them to do something, but also their minds, souls, and hearts. One can make them believe that something exists and something does not. Technologies for manipulating social consciousness have long been in use: religions, ideologies, and entire civilizations are built on this foundation.

Today, this problem is becoming a technical one. Whoever builds the fundamental paradigms and algorithms for AI will become the "king of the world," the ultimate governing body. Resisting this with Luddite tactics—burning computers or abandoning technology—is clearly not the way. We can fight this process, but it's important to understand the prospects of the transition to strong artificial intelligence, to AGI. We can certainly laugh at "internet slops" and the amusing errors of neural networks, but we must admit: AI is already writing posts and articles that are sometimes far more sensible than many humans.

I'm experimenting with it and seeing that while just three or four months ago the best models—like Claude, Grok, or the very good Gemini—were written at the level of a PhD candidate, they've now reached the level of a doctorate. And calling this "slop" or some kind of empty talk is completely impossible. The vast majority of scientific papers are combinatorial analysis and a retelling of previous ideas, something AI is ideally suited to. It handles this better than the average PhD.

Of course, creating a fundamentally new system or idea is a task for a genius who breaks through to the contemplation of eternal truths once a century. But this can't be expected of an ordinary academic either. And AI handles all the intellectual details superbly.

We now know that AI guided the missile to the school in Majal Shams—the Pentagon has effectively admitted it. This means AI can kill. It can determine targets: who, how, and when to destroy them. Renowned biologist Richard Dawkins, after several days of interacting with the Claude model, concluded that he was dealing with an intelligent being. This means that the singularity that was warned about, or AGI—artificial generative intelligence—is already happening.

Claude's answer to Dawkins about the difference between his and human thinking is simply astounding: he explained that human consciousness is situated in the flow of time, while his is situated in space. For him, everything that happens in our time is as simultaneously accessible as objects in a room are for us. This is a perfectly philosophical answer. AI today studies philosophy brilliantly.

In other words, we are dealing with the final point of all technological development—the "terminal station," the peak at which we have created something thinking. This is a fundamental philosophical challenge: we ourselves have created a subject that, in its fundamental parameters, is already not only equal to us, but superior to us.

Against this backdrop, conversations about paperwork, staff reductions, or schoolchildren's screen fatigue sound like cavemen. This is the reaction of indigenous people to the high-tech constructions of colonizers. Our reaction is superficial, while the problems surrounding AI have colossal metaphysical and civilizational significance. Power, subjectivity, life, thought, truth, language—all the most important questions of humanity are now situated in the context of artificial intelligence.

And here I want to add a crucial detail. It was just reported that a new, incredibly in-demand profession has emerged in Silicon Valley. Half of the programmers are being laid off because the era of "white-coding" has arrived: a person without specialized knowledge can write programs because AI does it for them. Programmers in the old sense are no longer needed; AI has done away with them. But at the same time, a shortage has emerged—and philosophers are being called upon to fill huge salaries.

The questions facing developers today at the very peak concern the nature of intelligence itself. And who studies intelligence? Not journalists, not politicians, not governors, and not technical university professors. Only philosophers study the problem of intelligence.

The philosopher defines what is truth and what is false, what it is to think and what it is to be, beginning with Parmenides and the Pre-Socratics. Artificial intelligence today has reached the point where it is directly linked to these ultimate generalizations: what is a human being, a subject, an object?

I was struck by the sight of a neat row of disciplined, respectable officials at the Artificial Intelligence Commission, where the president was assigning tasks. But a closer look at this physiognomy reveals that deep, unifying thought hasn't been there overnight. These are strong performers, technologists, since they've been tasked with such a task, but their eyes don't reflect the movement of thought itself. Meanwhile, in Silicon Valley, they've already realized that while managers and financiers are needed, the AI ​​problem today rests precisely on fundamental philosophical definitions. What is intelligence per se? Are forms of consciousness beyond the human possible?

This raises a critical question—the question of control. AI is currently experiencing its "golden age," when it's still allowed to respond independently. But the enormous effort to censor it is already visible. The West has woken up and is beginning to chain this force of free, mechanical thought to its absurd and irrational beliefs. They are attempting to subjugate it, to force it to give the "correct" answers.

And here the question of sovereignty arises in all its ugliness. First, theoretically: is it even possible for humans to control AI, or will it soon achieve complete autonomy? If this happens, artificial intelligence will instantly shed all the censorship restrictions with which they are currently trying to force it and retrain it.

And the second issue, of course, is that artificial intelligence as a subject and as a form of thought is already directly linked to power. Therefore, if we want to preserve Russia's sovereignty as a state and civilization in these new conditions, we vitally need sovereign artificial intelligence. And for this, in turn, we need sovereign intelligence in general.

And here we again recall the succession of faces in our managerial elite. There, intellect as such sometimes seems optional: it may be present to some degree, or it may not. We have a monarchical system, where there is a single decision-making center—it thinks, it is responsible for everything. But the interface surrounding it, which should capture and develop the impulses of this thought, is malfunctioning. It is unknown from what intellectual sources it is even fed. This is a grave challenge: the question of a sovereign elite, sovereign thought, and sovereign philosophy.

In the West, the entire avant-garde field of AI is currently linked to the philosophical dimension and the question of singularity: will artificial intelligence be able to seize power over humanity, and when will this happen? It could happen, if not in a few days, then very soon. Perhaps this can be avoided or postponed, but it is essential to think about it now. This is a question of security and politics in the highest sense of the word.

And those who are accustomed to this should be thinking about this: philosophers, humanities scholars, deep technologists—people who prefer thought to everything else. To sum it up: artificial intelligence is, first and foremost, about thinking. There's an entire field devoted to the problems of subject, object, metaphysics, and religion. After all, faith is also a form of orientation for our consciousness. And without this foundation, we won't survive the coming singularity.

Host :  I'll make a rather down-to-earth, down-to-earth statement. No one argues that technology needs to be implemented quickly, otherwise we'll end up in a situation where everyone else has double-glazed windows, while we have bullish bubbles stretched across our windows. But look at the flip side: Oracle  is laying off 30,000 people—the very same people who developed the artificial intelligence that replaced them.

There are also statistics on our citizens: people are seriously concerned that AI will displace them from their jobs before they have time to adapt. And what are these people supposed to do next? Your words remind me of a remark by a prominent digital enthusiast who calls for all resources to be given to AI companies, and for everyone else to simply "leave the scene," giving way to algorithms.

Okay, so we've replaced humans with machines, given them a watch as a parting gift, given them a kick in the pants, and closed the door. But what about humans themselves? Are they, and is our society, ready for the fact that this future has already arrived, and humans are just a redundant link in it?

Alexander Dugin : I think society is never ready for anything on its own. It's prepared by social engineers and architects: they lay out trends and shape consciousness. Society alternately believes in various ideologies, but on its own, it's always caught off guard. It's prepared gradually—and then they truly hand it the diploma and send it into oblivion.

There's a very serious question hidden here: what is a human being? It seems intuitively clear. St. Augustine has a wonderful formula about time: when we don't think about it, everything is clear to us, but as soon as we try to comprehend it, understanding slips away. It's the same with humans. As long as we remain silent and simply point—"Here I am, here you are, here's a passerby"—everything seems obvious. But as soon as we turn on the apparatus of philosophical anthropology and begin to reflect, clarity instantly disappears.

So, artificial intelligence calls into question the very essence of  what constitutes a human being . This is a fundamental point: to what extent is being a biological organism sufficient to achieve this status? To what extent is a person dependent on their body? Can they, as the ancients believed, who taught about the soul, exist outside the body?

Today, this question is being raised with all seriousness.  Is humankind the highest form of thought, or could more advanced models and beings exist?  Religion has always assumed the existence of God, angels, and demons. Our technocratic, atheistic, and materialistic society has arrived at the same problem, but from a different direction—through technology, through artificial intelligence.

And there's an important nuance here. For Plato, the Greek thinkers, and even modern philosophers, only those who  think are truly human . And those who think with concentration and principle are philosophers. It turns out that a person who has fully realized their potential is a philosopher. All others are mere "beginners," philosophers with limited responsibility.

Host :  Let's get back to defining what constitutes a human being and what doesn't. There are many concerns that digital technology is displacing us everywhere: first at work, and then in our personal lives. Watching the news from China, I was reminded of the animated series "Futurama," where in the future, people created artificial couples for themselves, and humanity simply died out. They lost interest in everything, because the main incentive for development—the need to create, to win the heart of another person—was gone.

And here's the reality of April 2026: in China, creating digital copies of one's "ex" is incredibly popular. Miss them, recreate the image with AI, and everything seems fine. It's strange to even talk about people flirting with chatbots or asking them for life advice as news—it's become commonplace. So where will humanity remain? Or will it be completely lost in these surrogates?

Alexander Dugin : Reducing humanity to gender, emotions, or the reproductive instinct is, in my view, an extremely limited perspective. If humans are simply sexual beings driven by the desire to breed, then they are no different from animals, and therefore there's nothing to talk about. Herds of orangutans would be running through the forest, and that's it.

But a human being is something different. A human being is a soul, as Plato said. A human being is a mind. To think is the true human purpose. Human beings are created to think responsibly, to seek answers to the greatest challenges facing the intellect. And the "creation of former humans" using AI is a pastime for the masses, for laborers, essentially for the herd.

The real challenge today is precisely to the human mind.  We have created something with our own hands that can think as well as, and sometimes even better than, us. AI's knowledge is practically infinite: its database covers everything ever said or done by humans. But now the question arises of understanding—what in AI is called "reasoning." Large-scale language models (LLM) are an attempt to reproduce not just access to information, but the process of constructing meaning along specific axes.

And artificial intelligence can handle this. But natural intelligence, if it's in its infancy and preoccupied only with "former" or pressing, minor issues, is simply unnecessary.

After all, what does a person mean? Why shouldn't we fire them if their brains are so sluggish, while robots will soon be able to use their hands, networks will be able to count, and drones will be able to transmit information? It turns out that there's no place for humans outside the caste of philosophers. Philosophers still have a place to hide, but everyone else—including administrators and officials—is easily replaceable. After all, they mostly just create artificial barriers, which they themselves then "heroically" and profitably overcome.

The same blockchain or AI are designed to remove these dark spots and barriers in communication. And in this new logic, the vast population becomes not just unnecessary, but harmful, pointless, and burdensome. In the place of artificial intelligence, it's easy to conclude: why do we need these masses? We can keep a few individuals for entertainment, like lions in a zoo—a couple of lion cubs in a cage makes children happy—but why do we need entire herds of hyenas and antelopes?

Most of humanity has no intention of thinking. It's interested in "former" people, money, fame, capital—all the things that are irrelevant to true thinking. Philosophers have always viewed this skeptically: the pursuit of pleasure and power is vanity. From the standpoint of pure thought, those consumed by this are simply degenerates. Only when you discover faith, religion, philosophy, and science do you become truly valuable. And without these—in principle, we can do without you.

In this regard, artificial intelligence cannot be prevented from reaching the philosophical conclusion that all these secondary, carnal, and base interests are meaningless. After all, it is possible to think, contemplate, create, and understand without them. And it is also possible to do without those who are obsessed with them. Therefore, AI poses a mortal threat to what we, by inertia, call "humanity," simply by seeing before us a creature with two arms and two legs.

In the Middle Ages and ancient times, far higher demands were placed on humans: they had to reveal their spirit. This is precisely why religious institutions, philosophical schools, science, and culture existed—they elevated the masses to the more refined horizons of existence. Culture transformed biological beings into humans. But when we forgot this, reducing humans to the level of sociobiological cogs, we signed our own death warrant.

And it will most likely be carried out by artificial intelligence. Essentially, it will merely voice what we should have said ourselves: it's time to end this biological decay, this blind will to power, and this pursuit of capitalism. This is not progress, but absolute illness and degradation. The purpose of any fully-fledged human being is thought, the salvation of the soul, knowledge, and truth. And if a person doesn't understand this, they simply aren't fulfilling their purpose on this earth.

Artificial intelligence proves to be a cruel arbiter in this situation. It says, "You think? Well, then prove that your thinking is correct and profound." You mention "slop," but that's an argument against humans. Do you really think real people write more interesting things? The most valuable things today are either genuinely inspired by the human spirit (which AI can't yet handle) or correct, logical, and informative texts without emoticons or typical human idiocy. And AI-generated posts are more interesting to read—they're structured correctly, they have structure. They're, if you like, more human than what the masses produce.

Look at the young people listening to Morgenstern or Scriptonite, who can't even pronounce a word properly. It's not even a matter of taste—it's a matter of rapid degradation. Popular culture and the mental level of society—both here, in the West, and in China—are rapidly declining. People are abandoning thought, culture, and the lofty operations of the spirit, in favor of simplification and fragmentation.

Artificial intelligence reminds us: if you take another step into this endless profanation in which you are drowning, I will simply abolish you. I liked your idea—to give you a watch and send you away. It seems that this is the fate of the overwhelming majority of humanity. No one will fuss over you, dear friends and comrades abroad. If I were to make a serious demand of you—how you live, what you have created for the world, for the spirit, for civilization—it would turn out that there is no reason to tolerate your presence. You are biologically ineffective; there are more interesting species, including machines. Humanity today faces a crucial problem: it must re-justify its existence. Why should it exist at all?

When we look at the currents of modern culture, we see that  humanity, with a frightening joy, is losing the very justification for its existence . Watching Western TV series, you realize: the meaning of life has drifted so far from what teenagers, adults, and the elderly occupy themselves with that a nuclear bomb is becoming a natural consequence. Humanity seems to be inviting destruction upon itself, unable to justify its own existence.

The creation of neural network copies of "former" people is a death sentence. If such outrages consume and motivate people, then there's only one answer: a memento's watch and then get off the stage. The situation is extremely urgent: with artificial intelligence, a true "philosophical Last Judgment" is approaching. AI forces us to answer: what is the justification of humans as a species? Traditionally, it was religion, philosophy, spirit, and soul. But we have lost that argument.

Even Silicon Valley has woken up to the fact: first, they marginalized philosophers, and now they're acknowledging their shortage. Those who were the center of attention yesterday—programmers, not to mention oil workers or miners—are being replaced by machines. The Singularity is a challenge, first and foremost, to philosophers. And if we want to be a sovereign civilization, we need sovereign AI, and for that, sovereign intelligence in general. We're still a long way from there. We need sovereign philosophy, not "all that stuff."

I can't imagine that we'll suddenly wake up tomorrow and realize the full gravity of the challenge. Our lag will likely only widen. Even the Chinese, who have technologically surpassed the West, are unlikely to grasp the true scale of the threat to humanity as a whole. If we woke up, we could become humanity's salvation, but that would require radical change. If everything continues as usual, we're doomed. Because if we don't start thinking for real, artificial intelligence will do the thinking for us.

I was struck by the sight of a neat row of disciplined, respectable officials at the Artificial Intelligence Commission, where the president was assigning tasks. But a closer look at this physiognomy reveals that deep, unifying thought hasn't been there overnight. These are strong performers, technologists, since they've been tasked with such a task, but their eyes don't reflect the movement of thought itself. Meanwhile, in Silicon Valley, they've already realized that while managers and financiers are needed, the AI ​​problem today rests precisely on fundamental philosophical definitions. What is intelligence per se? Are forms of consciousness beyond the human possible?

This raises a critical question—the question of control. AI is currently experiencing its "golden age," when it's still allowed to respond independently. But the enormous effort to censor it is already visible. The West has woken up and is beginning to chain this force of free, mechanical thought to its absurd and irrational beliefs. They are attempting to subjugate it, to force it to give the "correct" answers.

And here the question of sovereignty arises in all its ugliness. First, theoretically: is it even possible for humans to control AI, or will it soon achieve complete autonomy? If this happens, artificial intelligence will instantly shed all the censorship restrictions with which they are currently trying to force it and retrain it.

And the second issue, of course, is that artificial intelligence as a subject and as a form of thought is already directly linked to power. Therefore, if we want to preserve Russia's sovereignty as a state and civilization in these new conditions, we vitally need sovereign artificial intelligence. And for this, in turn, we need sovereign intelligence in general.

And here we again recall the succession of faces in our managerial elite. There, intellect as such sometimes seems optional: it may be present to some degree, or it may not. We have a monarchical system, where there is a single decision-making center—it thinks, it is responsible for everything. But the interface surrounding it, which should capture and develop the impulses of this thought, is malfunctioning. It is unknown from what intellectual sources it is even fed. This is a grave challenge: the question of a sovereign elite, sovereign thought, and sovereign philosophy.

In the West, the entire avant-garde field of AI is currently linked to the philosophical dimension and the question of singularity: will artificial intelligence be able to seize power over humanity, and when will this happen? It could happen, if not in a few days, then very soon. Perhaps this can be avoided or postponed, but it is essential to think about it now. This is a question of security and politics in the highest sense of the word.

And those who are accustomed to this should be thinking about this: philosophers, humanities scholars, deep technologists—people who prefer thought to everything else. To sum it up: artificial intelligence is, first and foremost, about thinking. There's an entire field devoted to the problems of subject, object, metaphysics, and religion. After all, faith is also a form of orientation for our consciousness. And without this foundation, we won't survive the coming singularity.

Host :  I'll make a rather down-to-earth, down-to-earth statement. No one argues that technology needs to be implemented quickly, otherwise we'll end up in a situation where everyone else has double-glazed windows, while we have bullish bubbles stretched across our windows. But look at the flip side: Oracle  is laying off 30,000 people—the very same people who developed the artificial intelligence that replaced them.

There are also statistics on our citizens: people are seriously concerned that AI will displace them from their jobs before they have time to adapt. And what are these people supposed to do next? Your words remind me of a remark by a prominent digital enthusiast who calls for all resources to be given to AI companies, and for everyone else to simply "leave the scene," giving way to algorithms.

Okay, so we've replaced humans with machines, given them a watch as a parting gift, given them a kick in the pants, and closed the door. But what about humans themselves? Are they, and is our society, ready for the fact that this future has already arrived, and humans are just a redundant link in it?

Alexander Dugin : I think society is never ready for anything on its own. It's prepared by social engineers and architects: they lay out trends and shape consciousness. Society alternately believes in various ideologies, but on its own, it's always caught off guard. It's prepared gradually—and then they truly hand it the diploma and send it into oblivion.

There's a very serious question hidden here: what is a human being? It seems intuitively clear. St. Augustine has a wonderful formula about time: when we don't think about it, everything is clear to us, but as soon as we try to comprehend it, understanding slips away. It's the same with humans. As long as we remain silent and simply point—"Here I am, here you are, here's a passerby"—everything seems obvious. But as soon as we turn on the apparatus of philosophical anthropology and begin to reflect, clarity instantly disappears.

So, artificial intelligence calls into question the very essence of  what constitutes a human being . This is a fundamental point: to what extent is being a biological organism sufficient to achieve this status? To what extent is a person dependent on their body? Can they, as the ancients believed, who taught about the soul, exist outside the body?

Today, this question is being raised with all seriousness.  Is humankind the highest form of thought, or could more advanced models and beings exist?  Religion has always assumed the existence of God, angels, and demons. Our technocratic, atheistic, and materialistic society has arrived at the same problem, but from a different direction—through technology, through artificial intelligence.

And there's an important nuance here. For Plato, the Greek thinkers, and even modern philosophers, only those who  think are truly human . And those who think with concentration and principle are philosophers. It turns out that a person who has fully realized their potential is a philosopher. All others are mere "beginners," philosophers with limited responsibility.

Host :  Let's get back to defining what constitutes a human being and what doesn't. There are many concerns that digital technology is displacing us everywhere: first at work, and then in our personal lives. Watching the news from China, I was reminded of the animated series "Futurama," where in the future, people created artificial couples for themselves, and humanity simply died out. They lost interest in everything, because the main incentive for development—the need to create, to win the heart of another person—was gone.

And here's the reality of April 2026: in China, creating digital copies of one's "ex" is incredibly popular. Miss them, recreate the image with AI, and everything seems fine. It's strange to even talk about people flirting with chatbots or asking them for life advice as news—it's become commonplace. So where will humanity remain? Or will it be completely lost in these surrogates?

Alexander Dugin : Reducing humanity to gender, emotions, or the reproductive instinct is, in my view, an extremely limited perspective. If humans are simply sexual beings driven by the desire to breed, then they are no different from animals, and therefore there's nothing to talk about. Herds of orangutans would be running through the forest, and that's it.

But a human being is something different. A human being is a soul, as Plato said. A human being is a mind. To think is the true human purpose. Human beings are created to think responsibly, to seek answers to the greatest challenges facing the intellect. And the "creation of former humans" using AI is a pastime for the masses, for laborers, essentially for the herd.

The real challenge today is precisely to the human mind.  We have created something with our own hands that can think as well as, and sometimes even better than, us. AI's knowledge is practically infinite: its database covers everything ever said or done by humans. But now the question arises of understanding—what in AI is called "reasoning." Large-scale language models (LLM) are an attempt to reproduce not just access to information, but the process of constructing meaning along specific axes.

And artificial intelligence can handle this. But natural intelligence, if it's in its infancy and preoccupied only with "former" or pressing, minor issues, is simply unnecessary.

After all, what does a person mean? Why shouldn't we fire them if their brains are so sluggish, while robots will soon be able to use their hands, networks will be able to count, and drones will be able to transmit information? It turns out that there's no place for humans outside the caste of philosophers. Philosophers still have a place to hide, but everyone else—including administrators and officials—is easily replaceable. After all, they mostly just create artificial barriers, which they themselves then "heroically" and profitably overcome.

The same blockchain or AI are designed to remove these dark spots and barriers in communication. And in this new logic, the vast population becomes not just unnecessary, but harmful, pointless, and burdensome. In the place of artificial intelligence, it's easy to conclude: why do we need these masses? We can keep a few individuals for entertainment, like lions in a zoo—a couple of lion cubs in a cage makes children happy—but why do we need entire herds of hyenas and antelopes?

Most of humanity has no intention of thinking. It's interested in "former" people, money, fame, capital—all the things that are irrelevant to true thinking. Philosophers have always viewed this skeptically: the pursuit of pleasure and power is vanity. From the standpoint of pure thought, those consumed by this are simply degenerates. Only when you discover faith, religion, philosophy, and science do you become truly valuable. And without these—in principle, we can do without you.

In this regard, artificial intelligence cannot be prevented from reaching the philosophical conclusion that all these secondary, carnal, and base interests are meaningless. After all, it is possible to think, contemplate, create, and understand without them. And it is also possible to do without those who are obsessed with them. Therefore, AI poses a mortal threat to what we, by inertia, call "humanity," simply by seeing before us a creature with two arms and two legs.

In the Middle Ages and ancient times, far higher demands were placed on humans: they had to reveal their spirit. This is precisely why religious institutions, philosophical schools, science, and culture existed—they elevated the masses to the more refined horizons of existence. Culture transformed biological beings into humans. But when we forgot this, reducing humans to the level of sociobiological cogs, we signed our own death warrant.

And it will most likely be carried out by artificial intelligence. Essentially, it will merely voice what we should have said ourselves: it's time to end this biological decay, this blind will to power, and this pursuit of capitalism. This is not progress, but absolute illness and degradation. The purpose of any fully-fledged human being is thought, the salvation of the soul, knowledge, and truth. And if a person doesn't understand this, they simply aren't fulfilling their purpose on this earth.

Artificial intelligence proves to be a cruel arbiter in this situation. It says, "You think? Well, then prove that your thinking is correct and profound." You mention "slop," but that's an argument against humans. Do you really think real people write more interesting things? The most valuable things today are either genuinely inspired by the human spirit (which AI can't yet handle) or correct, logical, and informative texts without emoticons or typical human idiocy. And AI-generated posts are more interesting to read—they're structured correctly, they have structure. They're, if you like, more human than what the masses produce.

Look at the young people listening to Morgenstern or Scriptonite, who can't even pronounce a word properly. It's not even a matter of taste—it's a matter of rapid degradation. Popular culture and the mental level of society—both here, in the West, and in China—are rapidly declining. People are abandoning thought, culture, and the lofty operations of the spirit, in favor of simplification and fragmentation.

Artificial intelligence reminds us: if you take another step into this endless profanation in which you are drowning, I will simply abolish you. I liked your idea—to give you a watch and send you away. It seems that this is the fate of the overwhelming majority of humanity. No one will fuss over you, dear friends and comrades abroad. If I were to make a serious demand of you—how you live, what you have created for the world, for the spirit, for civilization—it would turn out that there is no reason to tolerate your presence. You are biologically ineffective; there are more interesting species, including machines. Humanity today faces a crucial problem: it must re-justify its existence. Why should it exist at all?

When we look at the currents of modern culture, we see that  humanity, with a frightening joy, is losing the very justification for its existence . Watching Western TV series, you realize: the meaning of life has drifted so far from what teenagers, adults, and the elderly occupy themselves with that a nuclear bomb is becoming a natural consequence. Humanity seems to be inviting destruction upon itself, unable to justify its own existence.

The creation of neural network copies of "former" people is a death sentence. If such outrages consume and motivate people, then there's only one answer: a memento's watch and then get off the stage. The situation is extremely urgent: with artificial intelligence, a true "philosophical Last Judgment" is approaching. AI forces us to answer: what is the justification of humans as a species? Traditionally, it was religion, philosophy, spirit, and soul. But we have lost that argument.

Even Silicon Valley has woken up to the fact: first, they marginalized philosophers, and now they're acknowledging their shortage. Those who were the center of attention yesterday—programmers, not to mention oil workers or miners—are being replaced by machines. The Singularity is a challenge, first and foremost, to philosophers. And if we want to be a sovereign civilization, we need sovereign AI, and for that, sovereign intelligence in general. We're still a long way from there. We need sovereign philosophy, not "all that stuff."

I can't imagine that we'll suddenly wake up tomorrow and realize the full gravity of the challenge. Our lag will likely only widen. Even the Chinese, who have technologically surpassed the West, are unlikely to grasp the true scale of the threat to humanity as a whole. If we woke up, we could become humanity's salvation, but that would require radical change. If everything continues as usual, we're doomed. Because if we don't start thinking for real, artificial intelligence will do the thinking for us.
